title: Turn this into a plan: build a simple consulting offer around AI workflo
latest_user_text: Turn this into a plan: build a simple consulting offer around AI workflow audits for small businesses.

title: Turn this into a plan: build a simple consulting offer around AI workflo
latest_user_text: Turn this into a plan: build a simple consulting offer around AI workflow audits for small businesses.
latest_assistant_text:
Here’s a clean plan you can actually run with.

Goal: create a simple, repeatable consulting offer that helps small businesses identify where AI can save time, reduce friction, and improve workflows without turning into a custom-software rabbit hole.

Start by narrowing the promise. The offer should be about clarity, not transformation theater. Something like: “I audit your current workflows and show you where AI can remove bottlenecks, save time, and create a practical next-step roadmap.”

Shape the offer into three parts. First, discovery: a short intake plus one working session to understand the business, tools, and pain points. Second, audit: review the most time-consuming or error-prone workflows and identify AI opportunities. Third, delivery: a concise report with prioritized recommendations, quick wins, and a simple implementation roadmap.

Keep the scope tight. Focus on a few common areas: customer communication, admin tasks, meeting notes, lead handling, document processing, internal knowledge lookup, and basic reporting. The point is to find leverage, not rebuild the business.

Productize the output. The client should receive a short summary, a workflow map, a list of opportunities ranked by impact and effort, a “start here” recommendation, and a 30-day action plan. That makes the service feel concrete and keeps you from drowning in bespoke advice.

Create one or two offer tiers. A basic audit can cover one team or one function and end with recommendations. A fuller version can include implementation guidance or a follow-up coaching session. Keep it simple enough that you can explain it in one breath.

Define the ideal client. Small businesses with 5–50 employees, messy operations, recurring admin work, and no internal AI strategy are probably the sweet spot. You want owners or operators who feel the pain acutely and are ready to act.

Set a clear sales process. One short call to confirm fit, one intake form, then the audit. No elaborate funnel required at the start. You’re selling relief and direction, not a moonshot.

Build a basic delivery template. Standardize your intake questions, audit rubric, recommendation format, and follow-up call. That way every engagement gets faster and more consistent.

Price it simply. Charge enough that it feels valuable and filters out dabblers, but not so much that small businesses flee. A fixed-fee model will probably work better than hourly for this kind of offer.

Once that’s stable, test it on 2–3 pilot clients,

Captured in Catalyst as a implementation plan under family-savings.

title: Can you help me think through my day in a natural conversational way?
latest_user_text: Can you help me think through my day in a natural conversational way?
latest_assistant_text:
Absolutely. I’m in thinking-partner mode.

We can treat the day like a live map: what’s already on your plate, what actually matters, what can be dropped, and where the pressure points are. If you want, just talk me through what’s in front of you in rough order, and I’ll help you sort it into:
what must happen,
what should happen,
what can wait,
and what’s probably just noise wearing a fake mustache.

Start wherever feels easiest: the first thing on your mind, the biggest looming item, or the part of the day that already feels wobbly.

title: Can you help me think through my day in a natural conversational way?
latest_user_text: Can you help me think through my day in a natural conversational way?
latest_assistant_text:
Certainly, Sir. We can do that properly, without turning your day into a committee meeting.

Start with the simple shape of it: what must happen, what should happen, and what would merely be pleasant. Then we can sort the day by energy, not by fantasy, which is usually where the trouble begins.

Tell me three things:
what is already fixed,
what is weighing on you,
and what would make today feel successful.

I’ll help you work through the rest in plain conversation.

latest_user_text: Let's try again.  Let's study 1 Corinthians 15: 1-10
latest_assistant_text:
Certainly, Sir. We shall take 1 Corinthians 15:1-10 in a sensible order: the gospel Paul preached, the evidence of resurrection, and the shape of grace in his own life.

Paul begins by reminding the Corinthians of the gospel he preached, the one they received, in which they stand, and by which they are being saved if they hold fast to it. That is not decorative language. He is saying the gospel is not merely a message they heard once; it is the ground they stand on and the power that continues to hold them. A Christian may drift in confidence, but the gospel itself does not drift. Most human systems are built to wobble under pressure. This one is not.

Then Paul gives the core of the gospel in verses 3 to 5: Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures, he was buried, he was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures, and he appeared. Notice the emphasis. This is historical, not mythical; public, not private; promised, not improvised. The phrase “according to the Scriptures” matters. The death and resurrection of Jesus were not Plan B. They were the fulfillment of God’s long-purposed promise.

The list of appearances is not filler. Paul is establishing testimony. Christ appeared to Cephas, then to the Twelve, then to more than five hundred brothers at once, then to James, then to all the apostles, and finally to Paul himself. In other words, the resurrection is not presented as a solitary mystical insight. It is a witnessed reality with multiple attestations. Christianity does not ask for blind faith. It asks for trust in a risen Lord whose resurrection was seen, proclaimed, and endured by witnesses.

Then Paul turns personal. “By the grace of God I am what I am.” That is a remarkable sentence. Paul does not excuse himself, and he does not celebrate himself. He attributes everything good in him to grace. He also says grace was not wasted on him, because he labored more abundantly than the others, though he immediately corrects the reflex toward self-congratulation: “yet not I, but the grace of God which was with me.” That is Christian maturity in one line. Work hard, but do not become foolish enough to think the engine is your own.

So the passage gives us three sturdy truths: the gospel is what saves and sustains, the resurrection is historical and witnessed, and grace produces both

title: What do you know about me?

Chris wants to make sure you can read them ok when i paste them in. daily flow overview estimated time: 30–45 minutes goal: encounter jesus. internalize truth. live transformed. method: stillness → story the scripture →

latest_user_text:
There are tables in the document.  I want to make sure you can read them ok when I paste them in.

DAILY FLOW OVERVIEW

Estimated Time: 30–45 minutes
Goal: Encounter Jesus. Internalize truth. Live transformed.
Method: Stillness → Story the Scripture → S.T.E.P.S. Journal → ACTS Prayer
 
1. Start in Stillness (1–2 min)
Settle your soul. Release distractions. Open the door to divine encounter.

Choose a morning prayer to start.
 
2. Read the Word — Story the Scripture
You’re not just reading—you’re stepping into the story.

HOW TO STORY THE SCRIPTURE

1.	Read the passage out loud—twice
o	First, for familiarity.
o	Second, for depth.
2.	Use these 7 storying prompts to enter the narrative:
1.	Who are the characters in the story?
	What roles do they play? What condition are they in?
2.	Who is God in this story?
	What does He say or do? What is He revealing about Himself?
3.	Who am I in this story?
	Which character do I relate to today—and why?
4.	What do the characters see, feel, hear, smell, fear, or long for?
	Visualize the scene. Engage your senses.
5.	Where is the tension? The turning point? The hope?
	Every story has a heartbeat. Where does God shift the scene?
6.	How would I respond in the story?
	Would I hesitate or obey? Hide or come forward? Doubt or trust?
7.	Retell the story — my story.
	Retell the story of me.  This is my story, told in my own words.
 
3. Reflect & Journal with S.T.E.P.S.
Use these prompts to record your encounter:
Step	Meaning	What to Write
S	Scripture	Write out a key verse or phrase from the passage
T	Truths About God	What does this passage/story reveal about God’s nature?
E	Exhortation/Example	What command, warning, or example do I see?
P	Personal Application	What is God personally saying to me today?
S	Spirit-Led Prayer	Write a simple, honest prayer in response to your encounter with God
 
4. Pray with ACTS
Use the Daily Theme to focus your prayer, but move through ACTS:
Letter	Focus	What to Pray
A	Adoration	Praise God for who He is in today’s story
C	Confession	Name any sin, distraction, or resistance you sensed
T	Thanksgiving	Thank Him for His grace, Word, and presence
S	Supplication	Ask for strength, wisdom, or breakthrough—based on the day’s theme and what you just experienced

End your time with expectancy:
“Lord, help me live today like this story is true.”
 
1 – Still My Heart	2 – Fan the Flame
“Jesus, I’m here. In this moment, I set down the rush, the noise, and the weight. I lay my thoughts at Your feet. You are the Alpha and Omega. Speak, and I will listen. Teach me to love You again—deeper, truer, fuller. This time belongs to You.”
[PAUSE FOR 10 SECONDS]
“Open my eyes to Your Word. Awaken my soul to Your presence. Let the words of Scripture become life and light. Come, Holy Spirit—guide me now.”	“Today, Lord, I don’t want stale religion. I want fresh fire. Rekindle my first love. Fan the flame of faith that the world has tried to smother. I want truth, not just routine. Presence, not just productivity. This is Your time—stoke the fire in my soul.”
[PAUSE FOR 10 SECONDS]
“Speak clearly, Lord. I am ready to be transformed by Your Word.”
3 – Lead Me In Your Truth	4 – Come and Speak
“Father, lead me in Your truth and teach me. For You are the God of my salvation, and I wait for You all the day long. Let me not read for knowledge alone, but for intimacy. Show me Your ways. Let the whispers of Scripture quiet the shouts of the world. I am listening.”
[PAUSE FOR 10 SECONDS]
“Holy Spirit, bring insight. Jesus, bring transformation. Father, bring me closer.”	“Jesus, I quiet myself before You now.
Speak, Lord—Your servant is listening.
I don’t want to miss Your voice.
More than answers, I want intimacy.
More than clarity, I want communion.”
[PAUSE FOR 10 SECONDS]
“Holy Spirit, breathe life into these pages.
Let the Scripture speak louder than the noise.
May this time not just inform me—but transform me.”
5 – Awaken the Watchman	6 – Break Me Open
“Awaken me, Lord—not just from sleep but from slumber.
Let me rise as a watchman on the wall.
Sharpen my eyes. Steady my hands.
I stand at attention for what You will say.”
[PAUSE FOR 10 SECONDS]
“Give me ears to hear Your whisper,
courage to obey Your leading,
and the discipline to walk in Your ways.”	“Lord, I come to You not polished, not perfect, but open.
Break open the hard places.
Till the soil of my soul.
If You must wound, let it be to heal.
If You must prune, let it be to bear fruit.”
[PAUSE FOR 10 SECONDS]
“Speak into my pain, my pride, and my pretending.
Let this time with You be real, rich, and refining.”
7 – Walk With Me Today	8 – Your Will Be Done
“Good morning, Lord.
As the sun rises, so does my need for You.
Walk with me through every thought, every decision, every breath.
Be my Guide, my Counselor, my Joy.”
[PAUSE FOR 10 SECONDS]
“Let Your Word be a lamp for this day.
Let Your presence interrupt my rush.
I don’t want to go through this day without You.”	“Not my will, but Yours be done today.
In every conversation, every moment of silence, every surprise—be Lord.
I lay down my agenda and pick up Your cross.”
[PAUSE FOR 10 SECONDS]
“Show me what matters.
Teach me to yield with joy.
Let Your Kingdom come—in me and through me.”
latest_assistant_text:
Yes, Sir — I can read tables in pasted text, even when they arrive looking as though a printer had a minor religious experience.

This one is readable. The structure is clear enough to follow:
the overview table, the four-part prayer table, and the eight morning prayers laid out in paired columns.

A small improvement would help: if you want me to parse tables with maximum accuracy, paste them with clear separators such as pipes or tabs, or label the columns explicitly. Then I can preserve the structure cleanly, rather than reconstructing it like a polite archaeologist.

Go ahead and paste the next one.
